accept: Design note (or update to docs/commit-noise-design.md) addresses the two patterns explicitly: pre-claim metadata runs and same-field rewrite bursts
Quantitative analysis confirms or refutes the inbox-triage skew on at least one other downstream repo (e.g. aac-website) so we know it generalizes
Decision documented on amend-on-write vs explicit-batch-mode vs do-nothing, with rationale for whichever wins
If amend-on-write is chosen: design addresses the post-push safety question (don't amend a pushed commit) and concurrent-worktree interaction
payload.description:
Downstream-repo evidence (inbox-triage, a fresh project that uses act extensively): act-op commits dominate the git log at ~70%, but the dominant ops are NOT close. Close-time bundling (act-728d / act-a659) already works there; the residual noise is from pure-metadata ops that have no work commit to fold into.

Quantitative snapshot from inbox-triage main, 99 commits in a single ~6h window 2026-05-13 15:26 → 21:35:

- Total commits: 99
- act-op commits: 69 (~70%)
- Substantive commits: ~30 (~30%)

Breakdown of the 69 act-op commits:
- create (incl. create+1/+2): 32 — ticket filing
- close: 10
- tombstone: 6
- add_dep: 6
- add_accept: 6 (5 of these consecutive on a single ticket, act-65ae d24bd65..c46f361)
- claim: 5
- update_field / reopen: 3

Two structural patterns close-time bundling cannot help with:

1) **Pre-claim metadata runs.** `create` (+ optional `add_dep` / `add_accept`) happens during backlog grooming, before any claim exists. There is no upcoming work commit on the same ticket for these to fold into. Bulk-filing stretches in inbox-triage (e.g. 2c4f4bf 17fd066 ddca6d9 60ecaae) produce 3–4 back-to-back metadata commits each.

2) **Same-field rewrite bursts.** act-65ae's 5 consecutive add_accept commits are 5 separate "add an acceptance criterion" commits with no work between them. Each is a logically distinct op (different criterion text) but produces a redundant commit run.

The clean diagnosis: close-time bundling assumed every ticket goes claim → work → close, and folding happens at close. The data says a meaningful fraction of ops never have an adjacent work commit at all — they're metadata-only and happen in bursts during grooming.

Proposed scope (NOT prescribing the design; this is for investigation):

- Define a "metadata-op run" as: ≥2 consecutive act-op commits by the same author/session with no non-`.act/` changes between them.
- Investigate amend-on-write: when HEAD is a metadata-only act-op commit from the same session and the working tree is clean of non-`.act/` changes, the next metadata act-op amends instead of creating a new commit.
- Risks to consider: amends are dangerous post-push (would need to detect HEAD has been pushed and skip the amend); session identity needs a clear definition (writer node id? wall-clock window?); interaction with concurrent worktree agents writing to the same branch.
- Alternative: an explicit "I'm doing a batch grooming run" mode the agent enters, with one bundling commit at the end. Less magic, more deliberate; costs a CLI flag.

Out of scope here: moving ops to git notes or a side ref. That's act-8d67's territory and the trade-offs are different (loses PR-diff visibility, breaks fresh-clone simplicity, requires explicit fetch/push config).

Relationship to existing issues:
- act-208e (orchestrator-scoped bundling): related but framed around orchestrator mode B; this one is the broader "what about pre-claim and burst metadata" framing that applies even outside orchestrator mode.
- act-dfa5 (close+commit fold gap on aac-website): different failure mode — that's about per_session not folding when it should; this is about ops where there's nothing to fold into in the first place.
- act-6018 (closed) + commit-noise-design.md: the design note's recommendation didn't anticipate this skew toward metadata-only ops. Worth a follow-up section.

Source: another Claude session evaluating inbox-triage commit history at Andrew's request, 2026-05-13.
payload.title: handle pure-metadata act-op runs that close-time bundling can't fold (inbox-triage downstream data)
